abstract: The selective modification of nitrogen-containing heteroaromatics is a cornerstone of chemical biology and drug discovery. While recent advancements have introduced methods for expanding or contracting the skeletal frameworks of these heterocycles, techniques for the direct exchange of core atoms remain remarkably scarce. Here, we report a strategy for the $^{14}$N $\rightarrow$ $^{15}$N isotopic transmutation of several aromatic nitrogen heterocycles. This skeletal editing approach is initiated by the activation of the heteroaromatic substrate through $N$-triflylation, followed by a ring-opening/ring-closure sequence. This process, mediated by $^{15}$N-aspartate, facilitates the precise exchange of a skeletal nitrogen atom. A critical feature of this transformation is the formation of an isolable $^{15}$N-succinyl intermediate, which undergoes efficient elimination to reveal the isotopically labeled heterocycle. Conducted under mild conditions, this method provides access to diverse labeled pyridines and pyrimidines in high chemical and isotopic yields, offering a streamlined route to isotopic enrichment without the need for de novo synthesis.
